title: Tuberculosis — Pulmonary
oneLiner: Chronic bacterial lung infection (usually *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*) causing cough, night sweats, weight loss — if untreated, progressive respiratory failure and dissemination.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive haemoptysis** (Rasmussen aneurysm erosion into bronchus): sudden large-volume haemoptysis — not small streaks. *Single finding*: patient coughing up frank blood >200 mL, unable to clear airway, dropping saturations despite O2.
- **TB meningitis with hydrocephalus** (can present with concurrent pulmonary TB): headache, neck stiffness, decreasing GCS — *finding*: no fever initially, but focal neurology or papillodema. Do NOT assume TB is only pulmonary.
- **Miliary TB with ARDS** (haematogenous spread): diffuse bilateral miliary nodules on CXR, rapidly progressive hypoxia, high fever. *Finding*: CXR shows tiny 1–2 mm nodules throughout both lungs — this is a medical emergency.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms: **persistent cough >3 weeks** (often dry then productive), **night sweats** (drenching), **unexplained weight loss**, **haemoptysis** (usually streaks, can be massive), **fever** (low-grade intermittent)
- Key exam findings: may be normal early; later: crackles at apices, dullness to percussion (if cavity or effusion), cervical/axillary lymphadenopathy. In advanced: cachexia, clubbing rare.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)** — acute onset with purulent sputum, high fever, focal consolidation on CXR; rapid response to antibiotics.
- **Bronchiectasis** — chronic cough with copious sputum (often >100 mL/day). CXR may show tram-track opacities, CT shows dilated airways.
- **Lung cancer** — cough with haemoptysis, weight loss, but usually no night sweats; smoking history; CT shows mass, not apical cavities.
- **Sarcoidosis** — bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, cough, uveitis/erythema nodosum; no cavities, no AFB on sputum.
- **Fungal infection (e.g., aspergilloma)** — haemoptysis, but long-term cavitary disease or prior TB history; CT shows fungus ball within cavity.
- **Splitter:** **Sputum smear for acid-fast bacilli (AFB) or GeneXpert** — if positive, TB; if negative, reconsider alternative.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** sputum collection (induced if needed) for AFB smear and GeneXpert (molecular test detects *Mtb* and rifampicin resistance) — ensure 3 early-morning samples if possible.
- **Bloods:** FBC (lymphocytosis or anaemia of chronic disease), LFTs (baseline before drugs — TB drugs can cause hepatitis), HIV serology (mandatory — TB + HIV common co-infection), ESR/CRP (non-specific, elevated).
- **Imaging:** **CXR** (first-line) — typical apical upper zone (posterior segment) infiltrates, cavities (often right upper lobe), hilar lymphadenopathy, effusion. **CT chest** (second-line): when CXR non-diagnostic or suspect complications (empyema, bronchopleural fistula).
- **Special tests:** **Quantiferon/T-SPOT** (IGRA) or **Tuberculin skin test (TST)** — useful for latent TB diagnosis, but less helpful in active disease (can be anergic). **Bronchoscopy with BAL** if unable to produce sputum.
- Diagnostic criteria: **clinical + microbiological (culture or molecular confirmation)**: positive culture (6–8 weeks) is gold standard; GeneXpert gives rapid result (same day). **British Thoracic Society (BTS)** guidelines: any patient with symptoms + consistent CXR + positive AFB smear/GeneXpert = treat presumptively. Culture needed for drug sensitivity.
- **Never-miss test:** **HIV serology** — missing HIV means missed antiretroviral therapy (ART) timing, increased IRIS risk, and could change prophylaxis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If massive haemoptysis: call ICU/respiratory senior, cross-match, secure airway, consider bronchial artery embolisation.  
  - If suspected TB meningitis: CT head before lumbar puncture (LP) only if focal signs or ↓GCS; start TB treatment plus dexamethasone.  
  - If respiratory failure: high-flow O2, consider NIV/intubation.  
  - Implement infection control: place patient in negative pressure side room (or surgical mask if not available), airborne precautions until proven non-infectious.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Notify local public health (statutory notification).  
  - Start standard 4-drug regimen (see Drugs & Doses).  
  - Check baseline LFTs, renal function, vision (if ethambutol used).  
  - Monitor for drug-induced hepatitis (symptoms: nausea, jaundice, dark urine — stop drugs and check LFTs).  
  - Consider directly observed therapy (DOT) if adherence concern (e.g., homelessness, drug use).  
  - Arrange contact tracing via TB nurse.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Continue 4-drug phase for 2 months (if drug-sensitive), then 2-drug phase (rifampicin + isoniazid) for 4 months (6 months total).  
  - Extend treatment if cavitary disease or sputum culture positive at 2 months (to 9 months).  
  - Monitor for relapse: repeat sputum cultures monthly until negative.  
  - Manage drug-resistant TB (MDR-TB): refer to specialist centre, use second-line agents (e.g., fluoroquinolones, injectables, bedaquiline).  
  - Vaccination (BCG not recommended for active disease, but consider for contacts if negative IGRA).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Diet: no restrictions (encourage high-calorie, high-protein).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: yes, if immobile or risk factors (TB is pro-inflammatory).  
  - Observations: 4-hourly (NEWS2).  
  - Frequency of monitoring: weekly LFTs for first month, then monthly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (RIPE)** — all oral:  
  - **Rifampicin** — 600 mg (body weight <50 kg: 450 mg) once daily, 6 months total.  
  - **Isoniazid** — 300 mg once daily (give with pyridoxine 10–25 mg to prevent neuropathy).  
  - **Pyrazinamide** — weight-based: [check local policy] (typically ~1.5–2 g daily) for 2 months.  
  - **Ethambutol** — weight-based: [check local policy] (usually 15 mg/kg daily) for 2 months (stop if drug sensitivity shows fully sensitive).  
  - **Dose adjustment**: all four require dose reduction in renal impairment (especially ethambutol, pyrazinamide) — check BNF.
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - For isoniazid resistance: add fluoroquinolone (e.g., levofloxacin) and replace isoniazid with ethionamide (specialist advice).  
  - For rifampicin resistance: refer to MDR-TB regimen.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - **Rifampicin** — potent CYP450 inducer: reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, antiretrovirals, methadone, statins. Warn patient about red/orange urine (harmless).  
  - **Isoniazid** — caution in hepatic impairment, avoid alcohol, may cause peripheral neuropathy → give pyridoxine.  
  - **Ethambutol** — dose-related optic neuropathy: assess baseline visual acuity and colour vision; stop if vision change.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Hepatic impairment: avoid pyrazinamide; reduce isoniazid dose. Monitor LFTs.  
  - Renal impairment: ethambutol and pyrazinamide require [check local policy] dose reduction; rifampicin largely hepatic.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Rifampicin overdose** — acute hepatotoxicity, "red man syndrome", GI upset.  
  - **Ethambutol** — irreversible optic neuropathy if dose >25 mg/kg/day sustained.  
  - **All drugs** — omitting pyridoxine with isoniazid leads to peripheral neuropathy (common in malnourished patients).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - **Haemoptysis** — life-threatening; bronchial artery embolisation or surgery.  
  - **TB meningitis** — headache, ↓GCS, hydrocephalus.  
  - **Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)** — especially in HIV patients starting ART: worsening TB symptoms despite effective treatment (treat with steroids).  
  - **Drug-induced hepatitis** (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide) — stop all drugs, check LFTs, rechallenge one at a time.
- **Chronic:**  
  - **Bronchiectasis** post-tuberculosis — chronic cough, recurrent infections.  
  - **Fibrothorax / pleural scarring** — restrictive lung defect.  
  - **Multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB)** — caused by non-adherence or primary resistance; requires prolonged, toxic treatment.
sections.Prognosis: - With proper treatment (>90% cure for drug-sensitive pulmonary TB). Mortality <5% in UK (higher if comorbidities, MDR-TB, or HIV co-infection). Relapse rate ~2–3% if compliant.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check LFTs before starting RIPE** — if LFTs >2× ULN, discuss with TB specialist before initiating.
- **Always assess vision before ethambutol** — document colour vision (Ishihara plates) and visual acuity.
- **Always screen for HIV** — TB is an AIDS-defining illness; if positive, involve HIV team for timing of ART (usually 2–8 weeks after TB treatment start, to avoid IRIS).
- **Always notify public health** — within 1 working day (statutory requirement in UK/IE).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT start TB treatment without sputum samples** unless clinical suspicion is very high and patient unwell — you may obscure diagnosis and waste drugs.
- **Do NOT use steroids without ruling out TB meningitis** — can mask symptoms and worsen outcome.
- **Do NOT stop treatment early** — leads to relapse and drug resistance. Ensure DOT if adherence is questionable.
- **Do NOT forget drug interactions with rifampicin** — especially warfarin, OCP, and antiretrovirals.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** jaundice, dark urine, or unexplained nausea/vomiting (hepatitis); worsening cough with new haemoptysis; vision changes (blurred or colour blindness); fever >39°C despite treatment.
- **Follow-up:** TB clinic within 2 weeks to check sputum conversion and LFTs. Contact TB nurse for home supervision if on DOT. Follow-up sputum cultures monthly until 2 negative.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (respiratory reg / consultant) if:**  
  - Haemoptysis >100 mL in single episode.  
  - GCS <13 or new focal neurology (suspect TB meningitis).  
  - Hypoxic with saturations <90% despite low-flow O2.  
  - LFTs >5× ULN with symptoms.  
- **Call ICU if:**  
  - Need for non-invasive ventilation or intubation (severe haemoptysis, ARDS).  
  - Refractory hypotension or ongoing haemorrhage.  
- **Universal trigger:** NEWS2 ≥5 with any "Killers First" sign.

rawMarkdown:
## Tuberculosis — Pulmonary
**One-liner:** Chronic bacterial lung infection (usually *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*) causing cough, night sweats, weight loss — if untreated, progressive respiratory failure and dissemination.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive haemoptysis** (Rasmussen aneurysm erosion into bronchus): sudden large-volume haemoptysis — not small streaks. *Single finding*: patient coughing up frank blood >200 mL, unable to clear airway, dropping saturations despite O2.
- **TB meningitis with hydrocephalus** (can present with concurrent pulmonary TB): headache, neck stiffness, decreasing GCS — *finding*: no fever initially, but focal neurology or papillodema. Do NOT assume TB is only pulmonary.
- **Miliary TB with ARDS** (haematogenous spread): diffuse bilateral miliary nodules on CXR, rapidly progressive hypoxia, high fever. *Finding*: CXR shows tiny 1–2 mm nodules throughout both lungs — this is a medical emergency.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms: **persistent cough >3 weeks** (often dry then productive), **night sweats** (drenching), **unexplained weight loss**, **haemoptysis** (usually streaks, can be massive), **fever** (low-grade intermittent)
- Key exam findings: may be normal early; later: crackles at apices, dullness to percussion (if cavity or effusion), cervical/axillary lymphadenopathy. In advanced: cachexia, clubbing rare.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)** — acute onset with purulent sputum, high fever, focal consolidation on CXR; rapid response to antibiotics.
- **Bronchiectasis** — chronic cough with copious sputum (often >100 mL/day). CXR may show tram-track opacities, CT shows dilated airways.
- **Lung cancer** — cough with haemoptysis, weight loss, but usually no night sweats; smoking history; CT shows mass, not apical cavities.
- **Sarcoidosis** — bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, cough, uveitis/erythema nodosum; no cavities, no AFB on sputum.
- **Fungal infection (e.g., aspergilloma)** — haemoptysis, but long-term cavitary disease or prior TB history; CT shows fungus ball within cavity.
- **Splitter:** **Sputum smear for acid-fast bacilli (AFB) or GeneXpert** — if positive, TB; if negative, reconsider alternative.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** sputum collection (induced if needed) for AFB smear and GeneXpert (molecular test detects *Mtb* and rifampicin resistance) — ensure 3 early-morning samples if possible.
- **Bloods:** FBC (lymphocytosis or anaemia of chronic disease), LFTs (baseline before drugs — TB drugs can cause hepatitis), HIV serology (mandatory — TB + HIV common co-infection), ESR/CRP (non-specific, elevated).
- **Imaging:** **CXR** (first-line) — typical apical upper zone (posterior segment) infiltrates, cavities (often right upper lobe), hilar lymphadenopathy, effusion. **CT chest** (second-line): when CXR non-diagnostic or suspect complications (empyema, bronchopleural fistula).
- **Special tests:** **Quantiferon/T-SPOT** (IGRA) or **Tuberculin skin test (TST)** — useful for latent TB diagnosis, but less helpful in active disease (can be anergic). **Bronchoscopy with BAL** if unable to produce sputum.
- Diagnostic criteria: **clinical + microbiological (culture or molecular confirmation)**: positive culture (6–8 weeks) is gold standard; GeneXpert gives rapid result (same day). **British Thoracic Society (BTS)** guidelines: any patient with symptoms + consistent CXR + positive AFB smear/GeneXpert = treat presumptively. Culture needed for drug sensitivity.
- **Never-miss test:** **HIV serology** — missing HIV means missed antiretroviral therapy (ART) timing, increased IRIS risk, and could change prophylaxis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If massive haemoptysis: call ICU/respiratory senior, cross-match, secure airway, consider bronchial artery embolisation.  
  - If suspected TB meningitis: CT head before lumbar puncture (LP) only if focal signs or ↓GCS; start TB treatment plus dexamethasone.  
  - If respiratory failure: high-flow O2, consider NIV/intubation.  
  - Implement infection control: place patient in negative pressure side room (or surgical mask if not available), airborne precautions until proven non-infectious.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Notify local public health (statutory notification).  
  - Start standard 4-drug regimen (see Drugs & Doses).  
  - Check baseline LFTs, renal function, vision (if ethambutol used).  
  - Monitor for drug-induced hepatitis (symptoms: nausea, jaundice, dark urine — stop drugs and check LFTs).  
  - Consider directly observed therapy (DOT) if adherence concern (e.g., homelessness, drug use).  
  - Arrange contact tracing via TB nurse.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Continue 4-drug phase for 2 months (if drug-sensitive), then 2-drug phase (rifampicin + isoniazid) for 4 months (6 months total).  
  - Extend treatment if cavitary disease or sputum culture positive at 2 months (to 9 months).  
  - Monitor for relapse: repeat sputum cultures monthly until negative.  
  - Manage drug-resistant TB (MDR-TB): refer to specialist centre, use second-line agents (e.g., fluoroquinolones, injectables, bedaquiline).  
  - Vaccination (BCG not recommended for active disease, but consider for contacts if negative IGRA).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Diet: no restrictions (encourage high-calorie, high-protein).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: yes, if immobile or risk factors (TB is pro-inflammatory).  
  - Observations: 4-hourly (NEWS2).  
  - Frequency of monitoring: weekly LFTs for first month, then monthly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (RIPE)** — all oral:  
  - **Rifampicin** — 600 mg (body weight <50 kg: 450 mg) once daily, 6 months total.  
  - **Isoniazid** — 300 mg once daily (give with pyridoxine 10–25 mg to prevent neuropathy).  
  - **Pyrazinamide** — weight-based: [check local policy] (typically ~1.5–2 g daily) for 2 months.  
  - **Ethambutol** — weight-based: [check local policy] (usually 15 mg/kg daily) for 2 months (stop if drug sensitivity shows fully sensitive).  
  - **Dose adjustment**: all four require dose reduction in renal impairment (especially ethambutol, pyrazinamide) — check BNF.
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - For isoniazid resistance: add fluoroquinolone (e.g., levofloxacin) and replace isoniazid with ethionamide (specialist advice).  
  - For rifampicin resistance: refer to MDR-TB regimen.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - **Rifampicin** — potent CYP450 inducer: reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, antiretrovirals, methadone, statins. Warn patient about red/orange urine (harmless).  
  - **Isoniazid** — caution in hepatic impairment, avoid alcohol, may cause peripheral neuropathy → give pyridoxine.  
  - **Ethambutol** — dose-related optic neuropathy: assess baseline visual acuity and colour vision; stop if vision change.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Hepatic impairment: avoid pyrazinamide; reduce isoniazid dose. Monitor LFTs.  
  - Renal impairment: ethambutol and pyrazinamide require [check local policy] dose reduction; rifampicin largely hepatic.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Rifampicin overdose** — acute hepatotoxicity, "red man syndrome", GI upset.  
  - **Ethambutol** — irreversible optic neuropathy if dose >25 mg/kg/day sustained.  
  - **All drugs** — omitting pyridoxine with isoniazid leads to peripheral neuropathy (common in malnourished patients).

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - **Haemoptysis** — life-threatening; bronchial artery embolisation or surgery.  
  - **TB meningitis** — headache, ↓GCS, hydrocephalus.  
  - **Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)** — especially in HIV patients starting ART: worsening TB symptoms despite effective treatment (treat with steroids).  
  - **Drug-induced hepatitis** (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide) — stop all drugs, check LFTs, rechallenge one at a time.
- **Chronic:**  
  - **Bronchiectasis** post-tuberculosis — chronic cough, recurrent infections.  
  - **Fibrothorax / pleural scarring** — restrictive lung defect.  
  - **Multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB)** — caused by non-adherence or primary resistance; requires prolonged, toxic treatment.

### Prognosis
- With proper treatment (>90% cure for drug-sensitive pulmonary TB). Mortality <5% in UK (higher if comorbidities, MDR-TB, or HIV co-infection). Relapse rate ~2–3% if compliant.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check LFTs before starting RIPE** — if LFTs >2× ULN, discuss with TB specialist before initiating.
- **Always assess vision before ethambutol** — document colour vision (Ishihara plates) and visual acuity.
- **Always screen for HIV** — TB is an AIDS-defining illness; if positive, involve HIV team for timing of ART (usually 2–8 weeks after TB treatment start, to avoid IRIS).
- **Always notify public health** — within 1 working day (statutory requirement in UK/IE).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT start TB treatment without sputum samples** unless clinical suspicion is very high and patient unwell — you may obscure diagnosis and waste drugs.
- **Do NOT use steroids without ruling out TB meningitis** — can mask symptoms and worsen outcome.
- **Do NOT stop treatment early** — leads to relapse and drug resistance. Ensure DOT if adherence is questionable.
- **Do NOT forget drug interactions with rifampicin** — especially warfarin, OCP, and antiretrovirals.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** jaundice, dark urine, or unexplained nausea/vomiting (hepatitis); worsening cough with new haemoptysis; vision changes (blurred or colour blindness); fever >39°C despite treatment.
- **Follow-up:** TB clinic within 2 weeks to check sputum conversion and LFTs. Contact TB nurse for home supervision if on DOT. Follow-up sputum cultures monthly until 2 negative.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (respiratory reg / consultant) if:**  
  - Haemoptysis >100 mL in single episode.  
  - GCS <13 or new focal neurology (suspect TB meningitis).  
  - Hypoxic with saturations <90% despite low-flow O2.  
  - LFTs >5× ULN with symptoms.  
- **Call ICU if:**  
  - Need for non-invasive ventilation or intubation (severe haemoptysis, ARDS).  
  - Refractory hypotension or ongoing haemorrhage.  
- **Universal trigger:** NEWS2 ≥5 with any "Killers First" sign.
